skill.name: firebase-firestore-enterprise-native-mode
skill.description: Guide for setting up Firestore Enterprise Native Mode, designing data models, writing security rules, configuring indexes, and using Web or Python SDK examples.
skill.summary: Build and harden Firestore Enterprise Native Mode projects with provisioning, rules, indexes, and SDK guidance.
skill.author: firebase

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution, weak cryptography, sensitive file, and reconnaissance patterns. Review found these are documentation examples, Firestore rules syntax, CLI snippets, and security-hardening guidance rather than executable malicious code. The skill is publishable with a medium-risk warning because it can lead an agent to inspect a project, write Firestore configuration files, and run Firebase CLI commands.

medium_findings.title: Agent May Run Firebase CLI Commands
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for operational command guidance, but not malicious code execution. The skill documents Firebase CLI commands for database creation, deployment, emulator startup, index deployment, and rules validation; these can change cloud resources or contact Firebase services if executed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and relevant to the skill purpose. They are documented examples, not hidden execution paths, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Skill Directs Project File Inspection and Modification
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE semantic risk not fully captured by static analysis. The rules workflow tells the agent to scan the codebase, document findings, and create or update Firestore rules, which requires filesystem access and may expose project structure or change application behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file inspection and write instructions are direct and central to the workflow. They are normal for a Firestore rules assistant, but users should approve changes and review generated rules.

low_findings.title: Static Command-Execution Matches Are Markdown Syntax
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for hidden command execution. Most Ruby backtick and shell substitution matches come from Markdown code fences, inline identifiers, Firestore path interpolation, JavaScript template literals, or SDK examples rather than executable skill scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are documentation examples in Markdown. No executable helper script or automatic command runner was found in the skill files reviewed.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Sensitive-Key Matches Are Terminology False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for cryptographic weakness and credential exposure. The flagged lines refer to index ordering, markdown text, Firestore rule guidance, PII warnings, or hypothetical attack checks; no real private key, seed, certificate, or weak cryptographic implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The content is natural-language guidance and Firestore rules examples. I did not find evidence of embedded secrets, certificate material, private keys, or cryptographic code.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance and Hardcoded URL Matches Are Benign Context
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for malicious reconnaissance. The codebase scan instruction supports data-model discovery for rules generation, the hardcoded localhost URL is emulator UI documentation, and the URL regex is a validation example.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scanner patterns are present, but the purpose is local development and secure rule generation. No evidence found of data exfiltration or unauthorized network targets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cloud Resource Mutation Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes commands that can create Firestore Enterprise databases and deploy rules or indexes. Users should confirm project, database ID, and credentials before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are real Firebase CLI operations. They are expected for this skill but can affect cloud resources when run.

dangerous_patterns.title: Rules Generation Requires Broad Codebase Review
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill asks for scanning collection paths, queries, data models, and access patterns. This can expose project design details to the assistant session.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is explicit and relevant to generating accurate rules. It is a privacy and change-management concern, not malicious behavior.

content.user_title: Build Firestore Enterprise Native Mode Apps
content.value_statement: Firestore Enterprise Native Mode setup can be error-prone across provisioning, rules, indexes, and SDK usage. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured reference for secure setup and implementation.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to provision a Firestore Enterprise database with native Firestore data access.
Provides starter firebase.json, firestore.rules, and firestore.indexes.json configuration guidance.
Guides secure Firestore Security Rules generation with validation, ownership, RBAC, and attack review steps.
Summarizes Firestore document, collection, subcollection, and collection group modeling concepts.
Shows Web SDK and Python SDK examples for reads, writes, transactions, queries, and realtime updates.
Documents index density, unique indexes, composite indexes, and index deployment commands.
limitations: It does not automatically verify that generated rules match every production access pattern.
Firebase CLI commands require a configured Firebase project and appropriate credentials.
SDK examples are reference snippets and may need adaptation for application architecture.
Enterprise availability, pricing, and Firebase platform behavior should be checked in current Firebase documentation.

use_cases.title: Provision a New Enterprise Database
use_cases.description: Use the provisioning reference to create a native-mode Enterprise database and initialize rules and index files.

use_cases.title: Harden Firestore Access Rules
use_cases.description: Generate prototype rules with least privilege, schema validation, ownership checks, RBAC controls, and adversarial review.

use_cases.title: Implement Firestore Client Features
use_cases.description: Use the SDK examples to add document writes, reads, transactions, query filters, ordering, limits, and realtime listeners.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Firestore Enterprise Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me set up Firestore Enterprise Native Mode for my Firebase project. Show the required files and explain each step before suggesting commands.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Data Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my planned Firestore collections and subcollections. Recommend a data model for common queries, ownership boundaries, and collection group usage.

prompt_templates.title: Generate Prototype Security Rules
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this app structure and generate prototype Firestore Security Rules. Include assumptions, validation helpers, owner checks, and attack review notes.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Queries and Indexes
prompt_templates.prompt: Given these Firestore queries, identify required indexes, choose dense or sparse behavior where appropriate, and update the index plan safely.

output_examples.input: I need to create a Firestore Enterprise database and starter configuration files.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step setup plan with the database creation command, firebase.json guidance, locked-down starter rules, an empty index file, and deployment checks.

output_examples.input: My app has users, posts, comments, and likes. Help me write rules.
output_examples.output: A prototype rules strategy that documents assumed collections, validates fields, protects ownership and roles, limits public reads, and lists attack tests to run.

output_examples.input: Which indexes do these compound Firestore queries need?
output_examples.output: An index recommendation summary that maps each query pattern to single-field or composite indexes and notes when sparse or unique indexes may help.

best_practices: Review every generated security rule against real application access patterns before launch.
Run Firebase validation or dry-run deployment commands in the intended project context only.
Keep public profile data separate from private user data when writing rules and schemas.
anti_patterns: Do not publish rules that allow blanket authenticated reads on collections containing private data.
Do not let users create or update their own privileged role or permission fields.
Do not run Firebase CLI commands without confirming the active project and target database.

faq.question: Does this skill create a Firestore database automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides commands and file guidance. A user or agent must run commands with Firebase credentials.

faq.question: Can it generate production-ready security rules?
faq.answer: It can generate strong prototypes, but production rules need human review, testing, and validation against real app behavior.

faq.question: Does it support both web and backend development?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Web SDK and Python server SDK examples for common Firestore operations.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes Firebase CLI commands and file modification workflows. These are legitimate but can affect cloud resources and project files.

faq.question: Does the skill contain secrets or private keys?
faq.answer: No evidence found. Static sensitive-key matches were references to security guidance and hypothetical risks, not embedded credentials.

faq.question: Can it help with indexes?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains query support, dense and sparse indexes, unique indexes, and index deployment through Firebase configuration.
